title: INNER JOIN
description: Find the perfect overlap between two lists.

slides.title: The Strict Bouncer 👔
slides.content:
# The INNER JOIN

An **`INNER JOIN`** is the most common and strict type of join.

It only returns rows if there is a **perfect, absolute match** in both tables. If a user has never bought an order, or an order doesn't have a valid user, they are summarily rejected from the resulting grid.

slides.title: How to link it 🔗
slides.content:
## The ON clause

You declare the join in the `FROM` section, and use the word **`ON`** to explain *how* they connect mathematically.

`SELECT * FROM users INNER JOIN orders ON users.id = orders.user_id;`

The `ON` clause is you explaining the rules: "Please glue the users table to the orders table, BUT ONLY WHERE the user's id is perfectly equal to the order's user_id."

quizData.question: If Bob is in the `users` table, but he has zero orders in the `orders` table... will Bob's name appear in an `INNER JOIN`?

options.text: Yes, but it will show zero orders
No! Inner Join fundamentally requires a perfect match on both sides. Bob gets excluded.
Yes, the DB creates a fake order for him

quizData.explanation: INNER JOIN is the intersection of a Venn Diagram. It ONLY accepts records that actively overlap in both tables.

slides.title: Dot Notation 🚦
slides.content:
## Table.Column

When you use `JOIN`, you suddenly have columns from two different tables colliding! 

If both tables have a created_at date, `SELECT created_at` crashes because the DB asks "WHICH ONE?!"

You must clarify using Dot Notation: `SELECT users.created_at, orders.created_at ...`

quizData.question: Why do we use `users.id` in a JOIN instead of just writing `id`?

options.text: Because both the 'users' and 'orders' table inherently possess an 'id' column, so we must be specific or the DB will crash in confusion
Because 'id' alone is a reserved programming keyword and will crash
It's just to make the query look prettier

quizData.explanation: Dot notation (table_name.column_name) uniquely, explicitly identifies exactly which column you are referencing during complex joins.

slides.title: Try it Out!
slides.content: Join `students` to `grades`. The link is `students.id = grades.student_id`.
slides.codeSnippet:
SELECT *
FROM students
INNER JOIN grades 
  ON students.id = grades.student_id;
